content:
3 min read
1 hour ago

--

When I started the Refactory Academy JavaScript course, arrays were just “lists with square brackets.” By the time we finished building the Oyera Auto Service Bay dashboard, they had become the single most useful tool in my code — the thing almost every feature was quietly built on top of. If I had to pick one concept I’ve mastered the most this course, it’s **JavaScript array methods**: `.push()`, `.pop()`, `.find()`, `.filter()`, `.map()`, and `.reduce()`.
## Why arrays
It would be easy to say “classes” or “async/await” — they sound more advanced. But when I look back at the Oyera dashboard, almost every visible feature on the screen is one array method away from the raw data:
- The **”Cars in Bay”** counter isn’t a number I type in — it’s `services.filter(s => s.status === “In Bay”).length`.
- The **”Revenue Today”** card isn’t hardcoded either — it’s `services.reduce((sum, s) => sum + s.cost, 0)`.
- The **revenue-by-technician chart** groups every booking by technician using `.reduce()` into a small lookup object, then draws one bar per entry.
- **”Mark as Serviced”** finds the exact booking with `.find()`, updates its status, and everything downstream — the table, the cards, the chart — redraws itself.
None of that is complicated syntax. It’s the same five or six methods, reused constantly, because they map so naturally onto real business questions: *how many? which ones? what’s the total? give me a new list.*
## The shift in how I think about loops
Before this clicked, my instinct for “do something with a list” was always a `for` loop with a counter variable and an `if` statement inside it. It worked, but it read like a set of instructions rather than a description of what I wanted.
```javascript
// The way I used to think about it
let total = 0;
for (let i = 0; i < services.length; i++) {
total += services[i].cost;
}
```
```javascript
// The way I think about it now
const total = services.reduce((sum, service) => sum + service.cost, 0);
```
The second version reads like the sentence I’d actually say out loud: *”total is the sum of each service’s cost.”* That’s the real “aha” moment for me — array methods aren’t just shorter, they let the code describe the *what*, and let JavaScript handle the *how*.
## Where it clicked for good: `.filter()` and `.find()`
Two methods I reach for constantly now:
```javascript
// .filter() -> every matching item, as a new array
const carsInBay = services.filter(service => service.status === “In Bay”);
// .find() -> only the first matching item
const thisCar = services.find(service => service.plate === “UBA 123A”);
```
The distinction sounds small, but mixing them up used to cost me real debugging time — using `.find()` when I actually needed every match, or looping manually when `.filter()` already did the job in one line. Once the difference settled in, so much of the dashboard logic became “filter for what I want to count, reduce for what I want to total, find for the one thing I want to update.”
## Applying it beyond the dashboard
What sold me on array methods wasn’t the syntax — it was noticing how often the *same shape of problem* shows up. Counting how many spare parts are running low, listing which technicians are free right now, totalling a customer’s bill across multiple services — they’re all a `.filter()` or a `.reduce()` wearing a different outfit. Once I recognised the pattern, I stopped writing new loops for every new feature and started asking “which array method already does this?”
## What’s next
I still reach for a classic `for` loop when I genuinely need the index, or when I’m looping over an object with `for…in` instead of an array. But for anything involving a list of customers, vehicles, or services — which, in a garage management system, is almost everything — array methods are now my first instinct instead of my last resort. That’s the concept I’d say I’ve mastered most this course, and the one I expect to lean on the most going forward, especially as I start working with real API data next.
